answer.text: C + PV(K) = P + S, meaning a call plus the present value of the strike equals a put plus the underlying — any deviation from this creates an arbitrage opportunity
Call premium is always equal to put premium for the same strike and expiry
The sum of all call open interest equals the sum of all put open interest
Put-call parity only works for American options, not European

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Put-call parity (for European options): C + K×e^(-rT) = P + S. It links call and put prices through an arbitrage relationship. If the equation doesn't hold, arbitrageurs can buy the cheap side and sell the expensive side for risk-free profit. This relationship holds across all asset classes. **The Trap:** C ≠ P (they're related but not equal). Put-call parity is exact for European options; for American options, it becomes an inequality. It's about pricing consistency, not open interest. **Cross-Exam Context:** Put-call parity applies to equity (VIII Ch4), currency (I Ch4), and IR (IV Ch4) options. For currency: C + K×e^(-rfT) = P + S×e^(-rdT) (adjusted for two interest rates). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Put-call parity violations are rare but indicate genuine mispricing — conversion/reversal arbitrage strategies exploit these deviations when they occur.
